title: Random Matrix Spectra from Boltzmann-Weighted Lattice Ensembles
authors: Yaprak Önder, Abbas Ali Saberi, Roderich Moessner
abstract: We introduce a random matrix framework for studying statistical-mechanical lattice systems through spectral observables. Equilibrium configurations sampled from a Boltzmann measure are mapped to matrix ensembles whose covariance structure is inherited from the spatial correlations of the underlying model. This construction maps real-space correlation functions to a momentum-space variance profile, providing a direct bridge between statistical-mechanical correlations and correlated random matrix ensembles. We derive this variance profile in finite-correlation-length and critical regimes, and compute spectral moments within a Wick-contraction expansion. A complementary self-consistent description of the bulk density is developed using the resolvent formalism. These analytical methods are benchmarked against Monte Carlo data for the two-dimensional Ising model and three-dimensional Edwards--Anderson spin glasses. In both cases, the spectra evolve from the semicircle law at high temperature to model-dependent critical forms reflecting the structure of correlations. The framework, therefore, provides a quantitative spectral route to probing collective behavior in ordered and disordered statistical systems, while also defining a class of physically motivated correlated random matrix ensembles.
summary: This paper introduces a method to bridge statistical mechanics and random matrix theory by mapping lattice configurations to correlated matrix ensembles. It demonstrates that the eigenvalue spectrum of these matrices can quantitatively capture phase transitions and the evolution of spatial correlations.
main_problem: Developing a random matrix framework to study statistical-mechanical lattice systems through spectral observables and determining if eigenvalue spectra can serve as probes for collective behavior and criticality.
main_result: The authors establish that the eigenvalue spectrum evolves from a Wigner semicircle law at high temperatures to model-dependent critical forms that reflect the underlying spatial correlation structure.
method: The framework maps Boltzmann-weighted configurations to matrix ensembles using Wick-contraction expansions for moments and the Vector Dyson Equation (VDE) for the bulk density.
model_or_system: The study focuses on d-dimensional hypercubic lattices, specifically the 2D ferromagnetic Ising model and the 3D Edwards-Anderson spin glass.
key_observables: Eigenvalue spectral distribution (bulk density), spectral moments, spectral edge, and the variance profile in momentum space.
important_parameters: Temperature (beta), correlation length (xi), system size (L), and the anomalous dimension (eta).
main_assumptions: Assumes translational invariance, periodic boundary conditions, and uses a Gaussian/Wick approximation for off-critical regimes.
figures_summary: Figures show the variance profile V(k) across dimensions, the scaling of the fourth and sixth moments with correlation length, and the accuracy of the analytic spectral edge compared to Monte Carlo data.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the matrix construction, derives the momentum-space variance profile, applies analytical techniques (Wick expansion and VDE) to specific models (Ising and EA spin glass), and benchmarks results against Monte Carlo simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Behavior of V(k) plotted for d = 2, 3, 4, 5 for ξ = 0.1 (left), ξ = 1 (middle), and ξ = 100 (right). The propagator is normalized as shown in Eq. (III.25). In the ξ →0 limit, V(k) follows the Ornstein-Zernike form for all dimensions. As ξ →∞, the function has an IR divergence of the form 1/|k|d−4 for d < 4, and −ln(|k|) for d = 4.

FIG. 2: Distinct pairings (up to a symmetry factor) for the 4th and 6th moments of Wigner matrices. We have identified the matrix indices with the vertices and the matrix elements with the edges; pairings are indicated via lines connecting the edges inside the n-gon. Non-crossing pairings (4-ii,6-iii,6-iv in the figure) contribute at O(1) in the large-L limit, while crossing ones are suppressed.

FIG. 3: Fourth and sixth moments of the eigenvalue spectrum for T > Tc as a function of ξ for d = 2, explicitly computed from the numerical integration of relevant terms as shown in Eqs. (IV.6),(IV.7). Insets indicate the limit ξ →0. Note that initial deviations are O(ξ4), which indicates that the moments are stable to small perturbations to the semicircle law.

FIG. 4: Fourth and sixth moments of the eigenvalue spectrum of Ising Model (top) and Gaussian EA spin glass (bottom) as a function of ξ, computed from Monte Carlo sampling. ξ is calculated by fitting the matrix of variances to the relevant propagator form. The black dashed line indicates the analytical solution shown in Fig. 3.

FIG. 5: Scaling of the fourth and sixth moments of the eigenvalue spectrum at T = Tc for the Ising model as a function of system size, computed from Monte Carlo sampling. The results show good agreement with the scaling predictions from a moment method analysis, and the results for the fourth moment agree with [12].
